title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an urban beekeeper called Ethan Thorne talking about the challenges of keeping bees in a city environment. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an urban beekeeper called Ethan Thorne talking about the challenges of keeping bees in a city environment. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Ethan Thorne discusses the biodiversity of cities and the technical aspects of managing hives on rooftops.
extracts.audio_script:
Hi, I’m Ethan. When people think of bees, they think of rolling countryside. But actually, some of the healthiest bees in the country live right here in the city center. My fascination with bees didn't start with a jar of honey; it actually began when I noticed a [7]swarm of bees[/7] landing on a traffic light outside my office window. I was amazed that they could find a home in such a concrete jungle. I realized then that cities are much more biodiverse than we think.

Urban bees often produce more honey than rural ones. I’ve found that the [8]floral diversity[/8] in city gardens and parks is much higher than in the countryside, where massive areas are often covered by a single crop. In the city, people plant a huge variety of flowers that bloom at different times of the year, providing a constant food source for my hives.

The biggest challenge is where to put the hives. We usually use rooftops. I’ve found that [9]wind exposure[/9] is the most important factor when choosing a site. If it’s too windy, the bees struggle to land when they return with heavy loads of nectar. We use wooden screens to create a sheltered microclimate for them. It’s a delicate balancing act to keep them safe at such a height.

We are also using 'smart hives' now. I noticed that [10]internal temperature[/10] is the best indicator of a colony’s health. We have sensors that send data directly to my phone. If the hive gets too cold in winter or too hot in summer, the sensors alert me before the bees are in danger. It allows me to manage twenty different sites across the city without having to visit each one every day.

Pollution is a concern, but perhaps not the kind you’d expect. I’ve found that [11]exhaust fumes[/11] can actually interfere with a bee’s ability to smell flowers. They rely on their sense of smell to find food, and if the air is too dirty, they become disoriented. However, I’ve noticed that the bees are remarkably adaptable. They seem to learn which routes are the cleanest and stick to them.

There is a growing trend of 'corporate beekeeping'. Many companies want hives on their roofs to improve their green credentials. I’m worried that [12]overcrowding of hives[/12] in certain areas will lead to competition for food. If there are too many bees and not enough flowers, none of the colonies will thrive. We need to manage the 'bee density' of our cities just as we manage our traffic.

Looking forward, I believe that beekeeping will become a standard part of urban planning. I noticed that [13]vertical gardens[/13] are being built into new skyscrapers, providing miles of new habitat for pollinators. It’s a way of bringing nature back into the heart of our living spaces.

Finally, I’m currently developing a [14]training manual[/14] for city councils on how to plant bee-friendly public spaces. I want to ensure that every park and roundabout is a potential haven for wildlife. Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: ethan
